In 2014, EVE Online's Bloodbath of B-R5RB destroyed ships worth over 300,000 real dollars, and it started because one player missed a rent payment.
EVE Online holds the Guinness record for the largest videogame battle ever, with 8,825 players fighting in a single star system in 2020.
A real monument in Reykjavik harbor is engraved with the names of hundreds of thousands of EVE Online players.
In 2005, spies in EVE Online spent a year infiltrating a rival corporation, then assassinated its CEO and stole assets worth thousands of real dollars.
CCP Games employed a full-time PhD economist for years just to keep EVE Online's player-run economy healthy.
EVE Online players elect a real council, and the winners are flown to Iceland to advise the developers face to face.
World War Bee, one of EVE's biggest wars, was bankrolled by a virtual casino that paid mercenaries to besiege the Goonswarm alliance.
Band of Brothers, once EVE's most feared alliance, collapsed overnight in 2009 when a single defector disbanded it from the inside.
During EVE's Burn Jita events, players blockade the game's busiest trade hub and destroy billions in cargo just to prove they can.
Through Project Discovery, EVE Online players have classified real scientific data, helping researchers hunt exoplanets and study COVID.
EVE players have raised hundreds of thousands of real dollars for disaster relief by donating in-game currency through PLEX for Good.
EVE Echoes launched worldwide in August 2020, a partnership between CCP and NetEase that squeezed New Eden onto a phone.
In 2005, a World of Warcraft plague called Corrupted Blood escaped a raid and infected whole cities. Epidemiologists studied it to model real outbreaks.
Ultima Online's creator was assassinated in his own game in 1997, after a server reset briefly stripped Lord British of his invulnerability.
An economist calculated in 2001 that EverQuest's virtual world had a higher GDP per capita than several real countries.
World of Warcraft peaked above 12 million paying subscribers, more people than the entire population of Greece at the time.
The famous Leeroy Jenkins raid charge was staged. His guild planned the video, and the man himself confirmed the joke years later.
On June 6, 2006, a RuneScape glitch let one player kill and loot people inside a safe zone. Players still call it the Falador Massacre.
Old School RuneScape exists because players voted for it. A 2013 poll resurrected a 2007 backup of the entire game.
Anshe Chung became gaming's first virtual millionaire in 2006, turning Second Life property deals into a million real US dollars.
Club Penguin has a speedrun category where players race to get banned from the game as fast as possible.
Pac-Man ends on level 256, where an integer overflow scrambles half the screen into garbage. Players call it the kill screen.
A perfect game of Pac-Man scores exactly 3,333,360 points and means eating every dot, energizer, ghost, and fruit without ever dying.
Ms. Pac-Man began as an unauthorized speed-up kit for Pac-Man built by MIT students, which Midway then turned into an official sequel.
Space Invaders speeds up as you clear aliens because the 1978 hardware drew fewer sprites faster. The bug became the design.
Pong's first location test ended when the prototype broke down. Its coin box was jammed with too many quarters.
Computer Space, the first coin-operated arcade video game, came out in 1971, a year before its creators founded Atari.
Atari takes its name from the board game Go, where it warns that your stones are about to be captured.
Sega began as an American company called Service Games, shipping coin-operated machines to US soldiers stationed overseas.
Galaga has a legendary glitch. Let the last enemies of stage one shoot for long enough and no alien will ever fire at you again.
Polybius, the mind-control arcade cabinet of urban legend, never actually existed. Fans have since built real playable versions anyway.
One of the first video games, Tennis for Two, was built in 1958 on an oscilloscope by a physicist who had worked on the Manhattan Project.
Spacewar was written by MIT students in 1962 on a PDP-1, a computer that cost about 120,000 dollars at the time.
The first video game tournament was held at Stanford in 1972. The grand prize was a year's subscription to Rolling Stone magazine.
Atari's 1980 Space Invaders Championship drew more than ten thousand competitors, making it the first large-scale esports event.
The Magnavox Odyssey, the first home console, shipped in 1972 with plastic overlays you taped to your TV screen to fake color graphics.
Warren Robinett hid his name in a secret room of Atari's Adventure because the company refused to credit programmers. It became the first famous Easter egg.
Atari really did bury truckloads of unsold E.T. cartridges in a New Mexico landfill. A 2014 excavation dug them back up.
Activision was founded in 1979 by former Atari programmers, becoming the world's first third-party game developer.
The 1983 crash nearly killed gaming in North America, with industry revenue collapsing by around 97 percent in two years.
Nintendo was founded in 1889 and spent decades selling handmade playing cards before it ever touched a video game.
Mario was originally called Jumpman, and was renamed after Mario Segale, the landlord of Nintendo of America's warehouse.
Universal sued Nintendo claiming Donkey Kong copied King Kong, and lost because Universal had already proven King Kong was public domain.
Super Mario Bros hides a glitched Minus World that loops forever, reached by clipping through a brick wall in world one two.
The Konami code exists because a programmer found Gradius too hard to test. He forgot to remove his cheat before release.
A Game Boy that survived a barracks bombing in the 1991 Gulf War still powered on, and went on display at the Nintendo Store in New York.
The PlayStation began as a CD add-on for the Super Nintendo. When Nintendo publicly backed out, Sony turned the project into a rival console.
A surviving Nintendo Play Station prototype, the console Sony and Nintendo never released, sold at auction for 360,000 dollars in 2020.
GoldenEye 007 was built by a team of about ten people, and most of them had never made a video game before.
Ocarina of Time still holds a 99 on Metacritic, the highest score ever recorded for a video game.
Sega's slogan Genesis does what Nintendon't kicked off the fiercest console war of the 1990s.
During development Sonic was codenamed Mr. Needlemouse, a literal translation of the Japanese word for hedgehog.
Sonic 2sday, the launch of Sonic 2 in November 1992, was marketed as gaming's first global same-day release.
Sega's Dreamcast shipped with a built-in modem in 1998, betting on online console gaming years before broadband was common.
Sega's Game Gear devoured six AA batteries in roughly four hours. The humbler Game Boy simply outlasted it and won.
PlayStation 2 remains the best-selling console in history, with more than 155 million units sold.
Xbox got its name from DirectX Box, Microsoft's internal nickname for a console built around its graphics software.
Halo was first announced as a Mac game, unveiled on stage by Steve Jobs in 1999 before Microsoft bought its developer.
Nintendo's Switch has sold over 140 million units, overtaking both the Game Boy and the PlayStation 4.
Doom was so popular in 1994 that some companies banned it at work because lunchtime multiplayer was clogging office networks.
id Software released Doom's source code in 1997, which is why it now runs on ATMs, cameras, tractors, and printers.
Counter-Strike began as a free Half-Life mod made by two students. Valve hired them and bought the game outright.
Combos in Street Fighter 2 started as a timing bug. The designer decided the accident rewarded skill and left it in.
Mortal Kombat's Ermac was born from a rumor. Fans misread an error macros counter in arcade diagnostics as proof of a hidden fighter.
United States congressional hearings over Mortal Kombat and Night Trap led directly to the creation of the ESRB ratings board in 1994.
Organized speedrunning took off with 1996's Quake, when players spliced their best recorded demos into films like Quake done Quick.
A Dwarf Fortress bug once had cats dropping dead of alcohol poisoning after licking spilled tavern beer off their own paws.
Pokemon's MissingNo glitch could clone items, and Nintendo formally warned players about it while millions happily duplicated Rare Candies.
Mew was secretly slipped into Pokemon Red and Green by a single developer in the final weeks, using space freed by removed debug tools.
Pokemon exists because designer Satoshi Tajiri collected bugs as a kid and wanted city children to feel that same thrill of discovery.
Pirated copies of EarthBound let you play the whole game, then freeze at the final boss and delete every save file.
In Metal Gear Solid, the boss Psycho Mantis read your memory card aloud and mocked you for playing rival games.
You can beat The End in Metal Gear Solid 3 by saving mid-fight and waiting a week. The elderly sniper dies of old age.
Silent Hill's iconic fog exists because the PlayStation could not draw distant scenery. The limitation became the horror.
Grand Theft Auto began as a racing game called Race'n'Chase. A bug made the police wildly aggressive, and testers loved it so much it became the game.
Red Dead Redemption 2 simulates cold weather so thoroughly that a horse's testicles visibly shrink in the snow.
Rollercoaster Tycoon was written almost entirely in assembly language by one man, Chris Sawyer.
The Sims exists because designer Will Wright lost his home in the 1991 Oakland firestorm and became fascinated with rebuilding daily life.
World record Super Mario Bros speedruns finish in under four minutes 55 seconds, just frames away from the perfect tool-assisted time.
Ocarina of Time speedrunners manipulate memory with nothing but controller inputs, effectively reprogramming the game to jump straight to the credits.
At a 2014 charity marathon, a tool-assisted speedrun bot reprogrammed Super Mario World live on stage, turning it into playable Pong and Snake.
Games Done Quick speedrun marathons have raised more than 40 million dollars for charity since 2010.
At Evo 2004, Daigo Umehara parried fifteen straight hits with one pixel of health left. Fans still call it Evo Moment 37.
Blindfolded speedrunners have beaten Punch-Out and Ocarina of Time using nothing but sound cues and memorized inputs.
In December 2023, a thirteen year old became the first human ever to crash NES Tetris by simply playing too well. The game froze on level 157.
Tetris was created in the Soviet Union in 1984, and its inventor saw no royalties from it for over a decade.
Seeing falling blocks in your sleep after a long session is a documented phenomenon that psychologists literally call the Tetris effect.
StarCraft became a national sport in South Korea, complete with dedicated TV channels and pro players treated like celebrities.
Dota 2's International tournament offered a prize pool of over 40 million dollars in 2021, most of it crowdfunded by players.
Rickrolling evolved from an earlier prank called duckrolling, which bait-and-switched forum users to a picture of a duck on wheels.
All your base are belong to us, one of the internet's first big memes, comes from the clumsy English translation of the game Zero Wing.
Slender Man was invented in 2009 for a Photoshop contest on the Something Awful forums, then grew into global folklore.
Nyan Cat, the flying pop tart cat from 2011, was later sold as a crypto collectible for around 590,000 dollars.
Press F to pay respects entered everyday internet speech from a single button prompt in Call of Duty Advanced Warfare.
Twitch began as Justin.tv, where cofounder Justin Kan livestreamed his entire life from a camera strapped to his head.
Twitch Plays Pokemon let more than a million people share one controller through chat commands. The mob still finished the game in sixteen days.
Discord was built by a studio whose mobile game had flopped. The chat tool they made on the side became the real product.
Desert Bus, an unreleased Penn and Teller game about an eight hour real-time drive to Las Vegas, became a charity event that has raised millions.
Flappy Bird's creator deleted the game at its peak, walking away from about 50,000 dollars a day in ads because the fame overwhelmed him.
Wordle was built by Josh Wardle as a gift for his partner. Within months the New York Times bought it for a seven-figure sum.
Among Us was nearly abandoned for low player counts, then exploded two years after release to roughly half a billion players in late 2020.
Fortnite once deleted itself. For two days in 2019, millions stared at a black hole on stream while waiting for the game's next chapter.
Minecraft is the best-selling video game ever, with over 300 million copies sold. Microsoft bought it for 2.5 billion dollars.
Herobrine, the ghost said to haunt Minecraft worlds, never existed in the code. The patch notes still joke about removing him.
Grand Theft Auto 5 earned a billion dollars in three days, faster than any entertainment product before it.
Oblivion's infamous horse armor, sold in 2006 for about two and a half dollars, is remembered as the microtransaction that started it all.
Colossal Cave Adventure, the first great text adventure, maps a real Kentucky cave system its author had explored in person.
Gran Turismo's GT Academy turned videogame players into professional racing drivers, including one who stood on a class podium at Le Mans.
Dead Space famously has no on-screen health bar. Isaac's health glows along the spine of his suit, keeping the interface inside the game world.
